-JPZ21IAAAAJ.keywords: robotics, visual servoing, robot modeling and control, motion planning, sampling-based motion planning, multi-robot coordination, active perception, visual tracking, biomimetic aerial robotics, heterogeneous multi-robot task allocation
-JPZ21IAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Seth Hutchinson is a senior researcher and professor of robotics at **Northeastern University**, leading a research group focused on advancing the theory and practice of robot perception, planning, and control. His lab bridges rigorous algorithmic development with real-world robotic systems, emphasizing **visual servoing**, **motion planning**, and multi-robot coordination. Hutchinson's work is distinguished by its blend of foundational textbooks and influential tutorials alongside applied projects (from sensing and tracking to biomimetic flight), and it consistently emphasizes <u>robust perception-to-action</u> pipelines and <u>real-time control</u> for autonomous systems. His publications and textbooks serve as canonical references in the field, and his research agenda places particular value on *principled, implementable methods* that connect theory to practice.

---

## Research Areas
Hutchinson's research spans several core areas of robotics, with sustained contributions to both theory and systems. He is best known for foundational work in **visual servo control** (e.g., "A tutorial on visual servo control" (2002), "Visual servo control. I. Basic approaches" (2006), and "Visual servo control. II. Advanced approaches" (2007)), which develops control laws that close the loop on image measurements for manipulation and mobile platforms. Complementing perception-driven control, he has authored influential texts on kinematics and dynamics ("Robot Modeling and Control" (2006); "Robot modeling and control, 2nd edition" (2020)) and coauthored broad treatments of motion planning ("Principles of Robot Motion" (2005)). His work also addresses multi-robot coordination and planning under uncertainty ("Optimal motion planning for multiple robots having independent goals" (2002); "Path planning for permutation-invariant multirobot formations" (2006)), sensing and exploration strategies ("Planning exploration strategies for simultaneous localization and mapping" (2006); "Planning Sensing Strategies in a Robot Work Cell with Multi-Sensor Capabilities" (1989)), and practical performance issues such as real-time tracking and estimator design ("Real-time vision, tracking and control" (2000); "Estimating uncertainty in SSD-based feature tracking" (2002)). More recent and applied contributions include biomimetic flight platforms ("A biomimetic robotic platform to study flight specializations of bats" (2017); "Bat Bot (B2), a biologically inspired flying machine" (2016)) and long-duration autonomy and task-allocation for heterogeneous teams ("Robot ecology: Constraint-based control design for long duration autonomy" (2018); "A resilient and energy-aware task allocation framework for heterogeneous multirobot systems" (2021)).

## Notable Works
- "Robot Modeling and Control" (2006) — a widely used textbook that synthesizes kinematics, dynamics, and control for manipulators and mobile robots; it has become a standard reference for both teaching and research in robot modeling and control, and its later edition (2020) continues to influence curriculum and practice.  
- "A tutorial on visual servo control" (2002) — a seminal tutorial paper that established a coherent conceptual framework for image-based and position-based visual servoing, clarifying prerequisites from robotics and computer vision and guiding two decades of follow-on research.  
- "Principles of Robot Motion: Theory, Algorithms, and Implementations" (2005) — a comprehensive treatment of motion planning that connects rigorous theory to algorithmic implementation, widely cited for its role in making planning algorithms accessible and practical for robotic systems.

## Academic Background
Seth Hutchinson has a multi-decade academic career with research and teaching roles spanning leading robotics institutions. Over the years he has collaborated with and published alongside researchers at the University of Illinois and Georgia Tech, and he is currently affiliated with **Northeastern University**, where he continues to supervise students and lead research projects. Hutchinson is the author and coauthor of foundational textbooks and highly cited tutorials that have shaped robotics education and research; his work has accrued over 33,000 citations, reflecting broad influence across control, perception, planning, and multirobot systems. His publication record (from early work on sensing strategies and planning in the late 1980s and 1990s to recent contributions on robotic grasping and long-duration autonomy) demonstrates a sustained impact on both theoretical foundations and real-world robotic applications.

1GsJvtwAAAAJ.keywords: Cyber Forensics, Systems Security, Malware Analysis, Memory Forensics, Android Forensics, Software Supply Chain Security, Package Manager Attacks, Log-based Attack Reconstruction, Symbolic Root Cause Analysis, Web Application/CMS Abuse
1GsJvtwAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Brendan D. Saltaformaggio is an **Associate Professor** in the School of Electrical and Computer Engineering at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research group within the ECE department focused on systems- and evidence-driven security research. His work centers on **cyber forensics**, **systems security**, and **malware analysis**, with an emphasis on building tools and methodologies that help defenders and investigators reason about complex, real-world attacks. In particular, his group advances techniques for *memory forensics*, *log-based attack reconstruction*, and *supply-chain and software ecosystem security*, addressing challenges that span from low-level binary instrumentation to large-scale empirical measurement. Key technical themes in his work include <u>attack provenance and reconstruction</u>, <u>automated root-cause analysis</u>, and <u>abuse of web and package ecosystems</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Saltaformaggio’s research spans the intersection of systems research and applied security. He has developed methods for reconstructing multi-stage attacks from dispersed telemetry (e.g., the graph-based reconstruction techniques in "Hercule"), and advanced the state of the art in memory forensics and binary analysis (work on GUI and photographic evidence recovery from memory images, RetroScope-style screen recovery, and platform-agnostic binary rewriters like RevARM). He studies the privacy and information-leakage risks of mobile systems, including traffic-analysis attacks that infer fine-grained app activities over encrypted channels, and device-wide user-activity sequencing from Android memory images. His recent and highly cited work investigates ecosystem-level threats: empirical measurement and characterization of _supply-chain attacks against package managers_, large-scale studies of malicious plugins and web-app abuse used by malware, and automated remediation techniques for vulnerable open-source components embedded in application binaries. Across these threads, he emphasizes practical systems that scale to real-world workloads and support forensic investigation and incident response.

## Notable Works
- "Towards Measuring Supply Chain Attacks on Package Managers for Interpreted Languages" (2021, 255 citations) — A large-scale empirical study that quantified how package managers are abused by adversaries, demonstrating attack patterns and proposing measurement methodologies to detect and mitigate supply-chain compromises in language ecosystems.  
- "Hercule: Attack story reconstruction via community discovery on correlated log graph" (2016, 248 citations) — Introduced a correlated-log-graph approach to stitch dispersed log footprints into coherent multi-stage attack narratives, enabling automated attack-story reconstruction for intrusion analysis.  
- "Eavesdropping on Fine-Grained User Activities Within Smartphone Apps Over Encrypted Network Traffic" (2016, 148 citations) — Demonstrated that passive observers can infer fine-grained in-app user actions from encrypted network traffic, exposing privacy risks in modern mobile apps and motivating countermeasures for sensitive application flows.

## Academic Background
Brendan Saltaformaggio completed advanced graduate training in computing and has established a research program at Georgia Tech that bridges systems engineering and digital forensics. His publications appear in top security and systems venues and collectively have accrued over **1,855** citations, reflecting broad impact across academia and practice. His work has been supported through collaborations with industry partners and by competitive research funding, and he regularly mentors graduate students and postdoctoral researchers who pursue forensic, malware, and systems-security topics. He is an active member of the security research community, contributing tools, datasets, and empirical studies that have informed defenses against supply-chain compromise, mobile- and cloud-based abuse, and stealthy multi-stage attacks.

06hgT8gAAAAJ.keywords: Security, Operating System, Systems, Software Fuzzing, Concolic and Hybrid Symbolic Execution, Trusted Execution Environments (SGX), Cache Side-Channel Attacks & Rowhammer, Control-Flow Integrity, Memory Safety & Exploit Mitigations, Persistent Memory File Systems

1kW15d8AAAAJ.keywords: software engineering, science policy, articificial intelligence, software reuse, requirements engineering, software design, software maintenance, software engineering education, systems engineering, HGVS variant nomenclature

1vwuDM4AAAAJ.keywords: Cryptography, security, secure computation, zero knowledge proofs, garbled circuits, secure multi-party computation, oblivious transfer extension, private set intersection, oblivious PRF, private database systems
1vwuDM4AAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Vladimir Kolesnikov is a researcher in the College of Computing at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he conducts research within the broader **cryptography and security** research community. His work focuses on building *practical, high-performance primitives and protocols* for private computation, with particular emphasis on <u>secure two-party and multi-party computation</u>, <u>garbled-circuit optimizations</u>, and <u>privacy-preserving database and set-operation protocols</u>. He combines rigorous cryptographic design with system-oriented implementations and deployments, producing results that are both theoretically meaningful and *practically impactful*.

---

## Research Areas
Kolesnikov's research spans the design, optimization, and deployment of cryptographic techniques for private computation and secure outsourcing. He is a leading contributor to practical improvements in Yao-style garbled circuits (e.g., the free-XOR technique and follow-on flexible garbling work), as well as to efficient building blocks such as optimized arithmetic and comparison circuits for secure evaluation. His work also covers efficient primitives that enable large-scale private computations: oblivious PRFs and batched OPRF constructions used in private set intersection and union protocols; OT-extension optimizations for short secrets; and the design of protocols tailored to database query privacy and private biometric search (e.g., Blind Seer and related scalable private DBMS work). In zero-knowledge, he has advanced MPC-in-the-head instantiations for NIZK proofs with applications to post-quantum signatures and high-throughput ZK systems. Across these topics he emphasizes <u>concrete efficiency</u>, low communication overhead, and applicability to real-world deployments (smart grid transport and CPS messaging appear among his applied works).

---

## Notable Works
- "Improved garbled circuit: Free XOR gates and applications" (2008) — Introduced the **free-XOR** optimization that made XOR gates in Yao garbled circuits essentially cost-free, a breakthrough that substantially reduced communication and computation for many secure computation tasks and influenced nearly all subsequent practical GC implementations.  
- "A pragmatic introduction to secure multi-party computation" (2018) — A widely-cited survey and tutorial that bridges theory and practice in **MPC**, synthesizing progress and providing practitioners with a roadmap for building usable secure computation systems.  
- "Efficient batched oblivious PRF with applications to private set intersection" (2016) — Developed a lightweight, batched OPRF protocol enabling scalable **private set intersection** and other privacy-preserving set operations using symmetric-key techniques, improving performance for multi-party and large-dataset scenarios.

---

## Academic Background
Vladimir Kolesnikov has an extensive publication record across premier cryptography and security venues and has contributed both foundational techniques and applied systems. His work has appeared at venues such as Crypto, Eurocrypt, USENIX/Oakland, and ACM conferences, and he is an inventor on multiple patent filings related to secure systems and transport protocols. With a citation corpus exceeding 8,700 citations, his contributions (from theoretical primitives like Gate Evaluation Secret Sharing and free-XOR to systems like Blind Seer) have been widely adopted and built upon by the research community. He has collaborated across academia and industry on projects related to smart-grid security and scalable privacy-preserving infrastructure, and his papers are frequent components of teaching and practitioner resources in modern cryptography and secure computation.

_Z12v2IAAAAJ.keywords: Database Systems, Database Design, Schema Integration, Entity-Relationship Modeling, Association Rule Mining, Vertical Partitioning, Distributed Databases, Temporal Databases, Query Optimization, Genomic Databases

_ZIAy3cAAAAJ.keywords: human-computer interaction, information visualization, visual analytics, sensemaking, semantic interaction, interactive machine learning, analytic provenance, cognitive bias mitigation, large high-resolution displays, natural language interfaces for visualization
_ZIAy3cAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Alex Endert is an **Associate Professor** at **Georgia Tech**, leading research in the *Visual Analytics* group (see the Visual Analytics Lab: http://va.gatech.edu/endert). His work sits at the intersection of **human–computer interaction**, **information visualization**, and **visual analytics**, with a sustained emphasis on enabling human sensemaking through interactive systems. Endert’s group develops techniques that couple *visual interaction* with analytic models to support exploratory analysis, including frameworks for <u>semantic interaction</u>, methods for integrating **machine learning** into interactive workflows, approaches for capturing <u>analytic provenance</u>, and design strategies for using <u>large, high‑resolution displays</u> to externalize and augment cognitive processes.

---

## Research Areas
Endert’s research spans several tightly connected areas in visualization and human-in-the-loop analytics. He has pioneered work on *spatial externalization and sensemaking* (e.g., “Space to think: large high-resolution displays for sensemaking” and related studies of co‑located collaborative sensemaking and territoriality on large displays), and on interaction paradigms that let users steer statistical models through their interactions—most notably the family of ideas around *semantic interaction* and observation‑level interaction (e.g., “Semantic interaction for visual text analytics”, “Semantic interaction for sensemaking”, “Observation-level interaction with statistical models for visual analytics”, and “Interaxis”). He has also contributed broad community resources: surveys and position pieces on integrating **machine learning** into visual analytics (“The state of the art in integrating machine learning into visual analytics”), on provenance (“Characterizing provenance in visualization and data analysis”), and on human‑centered evaluation methods. Other recurring themes include mitigating cognitive bias in interactive analysis, mixed‑initiative recommendations, automated data‑insight generation and explanation (e.g., NLG-based data facts), model steering and interactive model construction, and design/evaluation of visual encodings and workspaces for complex analytic tasks.

---

## Notable Works
- “Space to think: large high-resolution displays for sensemaking” (2010) — a foundational empirical and conceptual study demonstrating how expansive spatial workspaces support external cognition, collaboration, and complex analytic workflows; influenced subsequent design and evaluation of large‑display visualization systems.
- “Semantic interaction for visual text analytics” (2012) and related papers (“Semantic interaction for sensemaking”, 2012) — introduced and formalized the idea that user interactions in a visual workspace can be interpreted to infer analytic intent and thereby steer underlying models, enabling tighter cognition‑computation coupling in visual analytics systems.
- “The state of the art in integrating machine learning into visual analytics” (2017) — a comprehensive survey and agenda that synthesizes approaches for combining interactive visualization with machine learning, clarifying design patterns, open challenges, and directions for human‑centered ML in visual analytics.

---

## Academic Background
Alex Endert is a senior researcher in the visualization community and an active faculty member at Georgia Tech, where he leads a lab focused on visual analytics and human‑centered machine learning. His publications (over 7,600 citations) include empirical studies, interaction paradigms, surveys, and system papers that have shaped practice and research in HCI and InfoVis. He has a strong record of mentoring students and collaborating across disciplines (including information science, machine learning, and domain applications such as cybersecurity and human trafficking analysis), and his work appears frequently in top venues for visualization and HCI. Through influential framework papers, methodological contributions, and tool development, Endert has helped advance how researchers and practitioners design, evaluate, and reason about interactive analytic systems.

_cAwG4gAAAAJ.keywords: Cybersecurity, AI, Blockchain, DSP, Computers, Internet of Things, Cloud Computing, Smart Contracts, Embedded Systems, Healthcare Informatics
_cAwG4gAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Vijay Madisetti is a Professor of **Cybersecurity & Privacy** at **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a multidisciplinary research group that bridges *systems engineering*, **blockchain**, **Internet of Things (IoT)**, and **digital signal processing (DSP)**. His work spans both theoretical foundations and systems-oriented implementations, with particular emphasis on <u>secure, interoperable distributed systems</u>, <u>scalable blockchain architectures</u>, and <u>real-time DSP for embedded platforms</u>. He maintains an active professional homepage (http://www.ece.gatech.edu/~vkm) and is a recognized leader in both academic publishing and technology transfer. His scholarship (total citations: **8628**) reflects a sustained impact across electrical engineering, computer science, and applied cybersecurity.

---

## Research Areas
Madisetti’s research portfolio is deeply interdisciplinary, combining classic signal-processing and systems design with contemporary problems in distributed ledgers and cloud-enabled services. In DSP and embedded systems he has contributed foundational treatments (e.g., the *Digital Signal Processing Handbook* and works on VLSI DSP and finite-wordlength effects) and practical prototyping approaches (virtual prototyping and rapid digital system prototyping). In networking and cloud systems he has advanced methods for workload modeling, scalable cloud analytics, and secure cloud-hosted services, including cloud-based frameworks for healthcare interoperability (cloud-based EHRs). In the blockchain and IoT space he has developed architectures and patents that address <u>industrial IoT integration</u>, <u>blockchain scalability and tuning</u>, <u>token and smart-contract mechanisms</u>, and <u>identity, ownership, and custody management</u> across multi-chain environments. More recently his work touches on privacy in machine learning (e.g., privacy-preserving fine-tuning of large language models) and payment-processing innovations combining fiat and tethered tokens. Across these topics he consistently emphasizes *practical, interoperable, and secure* solutions that can be validated on industrial and healthcare use cases.

---

## Notable Works
- "Blockchain platform for industrial internet of things" (2016, 1059 citations) — a seminal systems paper that proposes a blockchain-backed architecture for IIoT applications such as manufacturing automation, remote diagnostics, and supply-chain provenance, demonstrating how distributed ledgers can enable <u>trusted industrial data flows</u>.
- "Digital Signal Processing Handbook" (2010, 989 citations) — an authoritative reference consolidating theory and practice in DSP, spanning algorithmic foundations, implementation issues (including finite wordlength effects), and applications in communications and audio/video processing.
- "A cloud-based approach for interoperable electronic health records (EHRs)" (2013, 322 citations) — a multidisciplinary contribution that applies cloud-architectural principles to healthcare informatics, addressing <u>data interoperability</u>, secure storage/ access, and scalable analytics for EHR systems.

---

## Academic Background
Madisetti is a long-standing academic and technologist whose publication record stretches from foundational DSP and parallel/distributed simulation work in the late 1980s and 1990s through contemporary contributions in blockchain, IoT, cloud computing, and cybersecurity. He is a **Fellow of the IEEE**, a distinction reflecting sustained technical leadership. His output includes textbooks and handbooks used broadly in teaching and engineering practice, numerous highly cited conference and journal papers, and multiple patented inventions related to blockchain scalability, identity and access management, and tokenized payment processing. Based at Georgia Tech, his affiliations and collaborative work reflect strong ties to electrical and computer engineering, cloud and distributed-systems research, and applied cybersecurity; he has mentored students and collaborated with industry on prototyping solutions for manufacturing, healthcare, and financial technology applications.

_pdhnmwAAAAJ.keywords: Randomized Algorithms, Discrete Probability, Machine Learning, Markov Chain Monte Carlo, Glauber dynamics, Spectral independence, Mixing times, Gibbs distributions, Approximate counting, Graphical models
_pdhnmwAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Zongchen Chen is an **Assistant Professor** in the School of Computer Science at **Georgia Tech**, where he leads research at the intersection of **algorithms**, **probability**, and *statistical computation*. His group focuses on rigorous analysis of randomized procedures for sampling, counting, and optimization—especially the theory and practice of Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods. Central themes in his work include <u>Glauber dynamics</u>, <u>spectral independence</u>, and <u>entropy factorization</u>, together with algorithmic implications for spin systems, random constraint satisfaction problems, and continuous-sampling methods such as **Hamiltonian Monte Carlo**.

---

## Research Areas
Chen's research spans a cohesive set of topics in theoretical computer science and probability, with particular emphasis on the convergence and algorithmic utility of randomized processes. A sustained strand of his work develops and refines the theory of mixing times for local Markov chains (notably the Glauber dynamics) using tools such as *spectral independence*, *entropy factorization*, and contractive couplings; see, for example, "Optimal Mixing of Glauber Dynamics: Entropy Factorization via High-Dimensional Expansion" (2021) and "On Mixing of Markov Chains: Coupling, Spectral Independence, and Entropy Factorization" (2022). He has applied these techniques to concrete spin systems (colorings, hard-core and Ising models), exploring mixing behavior both in the uniqueness regime ("Rapid mixing of Glauber dynamics up to uniqueness via contraction", 2023) and beyond (work on random regular graphs and non-uniqueness regions). Complementing discrete MCMC, Chen has produced rigorous complexity bounds for continuous samplers—most notably sharp convergence rates for **Hamiltonian Monte Carlo** on strongly log-concave targets ("Optimal Convergence Rate of Hamiltonian Monte Carlo for Strongly Logconcave Distributions", 2022). Other strands include algorithmic counting and sampling for combinatorial structures (b-matchings, colorings on random graphs), lower bounds and hardness results for identity testing and planted structures, and connections between polynomial stability and algorithmic spectral independence (Holant-type applications).

---

## Notable Works
- "Optimal Mixing of Glauber Dynamics: Entropy Factorization via High-Dimensional Expansion" (2021) — established O(n log n) mixing-time bounds for single-site Glauber dynamics in broad settings by combining spectral independence with entropy factorization techniques and high-dimensional expansion.
- "Rapid mixing of Glauber dynamics up to uniqueness via contraction" (2023) — provided a contraction-based framework establishing rapid mixing for antiferromagnetic 2-spin systems up to the tree uniqueness threshold, unifying analysis across hardcore and antiferromagnetic Ising-type models.
- "On Mixing of Markov Chains: Coupling, Spectral Independence, and Entropy Factorization" (2022) — showed that contractive couplings imply optimal bounds on both mixing times and modified log-Sobolev constants for a wide class of local Markov chains, clarifying the relationships between coupling, spectral independence, and entropy methods.

---

## Academic Background
Chen trained in theoretical computer science and probability before joining Georgia Tech, following the common trajectory of doctoral and postdoctoral work in algorithms and random processes. Over the past decade he has published extensively on MCMC theory, sampling and counting algorithms, and probabilistic combinatorics; his body of work has accrued close to **952 citations**, reflecting strong impact across the algorithms and probability communities. He has collaborated with leading researchers in randomized algorithms and statistical physics, contributed both foundational theory and algorithmic applications, and maintains an active research group at Georgia Tech (see his homepage for publications and ongoing projects). His research has appeared in top theory venues and has influenced subsequent work on spectral independence, entropy methods, and rigorous analyses of sampling algorithms.
